headline: India's First Homemade Hydrogen Train Signals a Cleaner Railway Future
meta_description: India launches its first domestically built hydrogen-powered train in Haryana, marking a major step toward sustainable railways and net-zero emissions.
summary_short: India has unveiled its first hydrogen-powered train built entirely in the country, operating in Haryana and carrying up to 2,600 passengers.
body:
On Friday, India unveiled a milestone in sustainable transport: the nation's first domestically manufactured hydrogen-powered train, inaugurated at Jind railway station in Haryana. The "NaMo Green Rail," named after Prime Minister Narendra Modi, represents a tangible shift toward cleaner energy across India's extensive rail network.

The train comprises two hydrogen-powered driving cars and eight passenger coaches, capable of reaching speeds up to 75 kilometers per hour while accommodating approximately 2,600 travelers. What makes this achievement particularly significant is that it goes beyond simply adopting existing technology—India has built it at home, strengthening domestic capacity in green energy infrastructure.

Unlike conventional diesel trains, hydrogen fuel cells work by combining hydrogen and oxygen to generate electricity, with water vapor as the only direct emission. The pilot project includes dedicated hydrogen storage and refueling infrastructure, allowing Indian Railways to thoroughly test this technology across routes that aren't fully electrified.

This launch reflects India's commitment to its net-zero emissions target by 2070. As several other nations have already demonstrated, hydrogen offers a practical alternative to diesel on select routes, particularly where rail electrification remains incomplete. For India's vast railway system, this domestically built solution opens a promising pathway forward.
